title: A Roadside Stand by Robert Frost
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Economic inequality, exploitation of the rural poor, false promises of government, and the city-village divide.

notes.heading: Key Literary Devices
points: Personification: “The polished traffic passed with a mind ahead”

summary:
This poem highlights the gap between rich city people and poor rural people. The roadside stand is a small shed built by poor villagers on a highway to sell their local produce. They hope the passing cars of rich city-dwellers will stop and buy something. The villagers do not want charity; they want to earn money so they can live like the city people they see in movies.

But nobody stops. Cars only slow down to complain about the ugly signs that spoil the scenery, to ask for directions, or to ask for gas. The stand sells wild berries and golden squash, but the rich people do not value them. The villagers wait with childish longing, hoping for some city money that could change their lives.

Frost criticizes the greedy good-doers and political parties who pretend to help the poor. They move villagers to cities near theatres and stores, promising comfort and care. But this actually destroys the villagers’ old way of life. These so-called benefactors swarm over their lives, and the villagers lose their peace and independence.

The poet feels deep pain watching this. He says the news claims that these people will be put at ease, but he wonders how they can be at ease when they are uprooted. At one point, he even thinks it might be better if their pain ended at once, but then he checks himself because he knows that thought is cruel. He hopes someone will truly help the poor by stopping and buying from them.
